reasoning: The Chicago Fed's National Financial Conditions Risk Subindex is currently negative (-0.593), indicating looser/lower than average risk. Financial conditions indexes generally exhibit both momentum in the short term and mean reversion in the medium-to-long term. Because the index often declines slowly but spikes suddenly during risk-off events, the weekly probability of an increase may be around or slightly below 50%. However, given its starting level below zero, longer horizons introduce a slight mean-reverting drift upwards towards the historical mean of zero. Due to a purely zero-shot setting with no external search capability allowed on this turn, estimating the direction of near-term moves is essentially a coin toss based on the random-walk nature of financial markets. I therefore assign probabilities very close to 50%, increasing slightly for longer horizons to capture mean reversion from the currently negative value.
